meta.title: Lunar Lander GNC Failure Memory — structured, cited knowledge base
meta.compiled_by: Agentic extraction pipeline (orchestrator + research subagents), human-reviewed
meta.scope: Robotic soft-landing attempts on the Moon, Chang'e 3 (Dec 2013) through Hakuto-R Mission 2 (Jun 2025).
meta.counting_note: 15 attempts are recorded here. 'Success rate ~50%' depends on the counting rule: of these 15, 7 are clear successes (Chang'e 3/4/5/6, Chandrayaan-3, Blue Ghost M1) plus 2 partial successes that landed but tipped (SLIM, IM-1). Treating the 2 tip-overs and Peregrine (no landing attempted) as non-soft-landings gives 7/15 fully successful (~47%); counting the partials as successes gives 9/15 (~60%). External anchors: J. McDowell census ~43% (23/53 all-time); NASA CLPS planning figure 'roughly 50%'. State the rule explicitly when quoting a number.

outcome_classes.SUCCESS: Soft landing achieved, upright, mission proceeded as planned
outcome_classes.TIPPED_SURVIVED: Landed but ended in off-nominal attitude; returned some data (partial success)
outcome_classes.TIPPED_LOST: Landed but off-nominal attitude led to early loss of mission
outcome_classes.LOST: Loss of vehicle during descent (crash) or pre-landing maneuver
outcome_classes.NO_LANDING_ATTEMPT: Vehicle failed before a landing was attempted

meta.ility_axis_source: de Weck, Ross & Rhodes (CESUN 2012). Survivability = ability to minimize the impact of a finite-duration disturbance on value delivery. Robustness = ability to maintain specified parameters under changing internal/external forces.
meta.disclaimer: Failure causes vary in source quality. Each record carries a confidence level and a primary source. Several causes rest on agency/company statements or press reporting rather than full published mishap reports; these are marked. Do not overstate root causes that the responsible organizations themselves hedged.

missions.flight_phase: Autonomous powered descent (7-phase), hover hazard-avoidance at ~100 m
missions.sensor_or_actuator: Strapdown IMU + laser altimeter + microwave radar altimeter/velocimeter; 16-beam laser 3D imaging sensor (L3DIS) for hover hazard scan; optical hazard camera
missions.proximate_cause: None — first lunar soft landing since 1976.
missions.contributing_logic: Coarse-to-fine hazard avoidance: optical camera flags >1 m hazards during approach and picks the target; lander then hovers at ~100 m while L3DIS (range accuracy <0.15 m) builds a point cloud to detect >20 cm rocks before a final precise avoidance maneuver.
missions.propagation_chain: Fused multi-sensor navigation + hover-and-scan hazard avoidance -> safe site selection -> soft landing (Mare Imbrium).
missions.good_practices: Sensor diversity (inertial + laser + microwave radar); explicit hover-and-scan phase with dedicated 3D terrain sensor; autonomous hazard detection and avoidance.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Dissimilar redundant ranging + dedicated terrain sensing + hover margin
missions.ility: Robustness (held parameters through off-nominal terrain) + survivability margin
missions.key_quote: Coarse hazard avoidance via optical camera, then hover at ~100 m with a 16-beam laser 3D imaging sensor for fine obstacle detection.

missions.flight_phase: Fully autonomous descent (no Earth real-time link on far side); hover + hazard avoidance
missions.sensor_or_actuator: Inherited Chang'e-3 GNC stack; 7500 N throttleable engine; autonomous hazard detect-and-avoid + hover imaging
missions.proximate_cause: None — first far-side landing (Von Karman crater).
missions.contributing_logic: Because no real-time ground guidance is possible on the far side, descent was completely autonomous: main braking -> attitude adjust -> vertical descent -> hover + hazard avoidance -> slow descent.
missions.propagation_chain: Reuse of validated autonomous GNC architecture -> autonomous safe-site selection far-side -> soft landing.
missions.good_practices: Reuse of a flight-proven, mature autonomous GNC stack (commonality); designed for full autonomy without ground intervention.
missions.ility: Robustness via commonality (reuse of validated design)
missions.key_quote: Descent was completely autonomous, reusing the Chang'e-3 hazard-detect-and-avoid and hover-imaging architecture.

missions.sensor_or_actuator: Inertial Measurement Unit (one of two IMUs); main engine
missions.proximate_cause: One of two IMUs spontaneously failed at ~14 km. A ground command to reset/restart the failed unit triggered a flight-computer reboot; during the reboot the active IMU's data feed was also interrupted, the computer read this as a navigation fault and commanded a protective main-engine shutdown. Re-ignition attempts could not recover altitude.
missions.contributing_logic: Software treated any gap in accelerometer data as a navigation fault requiring reboot; reboot logic then shut the main engine. No latching/grace-period kept propulsion alive through a reboot, and no isolation existed between an IMU recovery command and the engine-control path.
missions.propagation_chain: IMU2 hardware failure -> ground command to reset IMU2 -> flight-computer reboot -> momentary loss of all IMU data -> fault logic commands engine shutdown -> 5-6 restart attempts fail -> impact (~500 km/h).
missions.design_smell: A ground-commanded sensor-recovery action can trigger a computer reboot that shuts propulsion; no isolation between sensor management and engine control; restart cascade never validated under concurrent hardware-failure + telecommand.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Isolate sensor-recovery from propulsion control; latch engine through reboots; validate fault-management under combined faults
missions.ility: Loss of survivability — a finite-duration disturbance (IMU glitch + reboot) was not contained and propagated to total loss
missions.key_quote: The command didn't directly cause the main engine to turn off, but rather caused the computer to restart and things carried on from there. — SpaceIL CEO Ido Anteby
missions.source_title: 5-6 attempts to restart Israeli moon lander's engine failed, investigation finds (Times of Israel)
missions.confidence_note: Restart/reboot cascade confirmed by SpaceIL first-phase investigation and CEO statement; the reason the original IMU failed was never published.

missions.proximate_cause: Nominal trajectory from 30 km to ~2.2 km, then a deviation at 2.1 km. Government statements attribute the crash to a problem with braking thrusters during fine braking, linked to a software glitch; communication lost at 2.1 km.
missions.contributing_logic: An internal ISRO report cited a last-minute software glitch in the guidance software. The fine-braking phase used a different thruster configuration than rough braking; the controlling software did not execute as designed, and the failure mode was not exercised in ground trials.
missions.propagation_chain: Software glitch in fine-braking guidance -> incorrect thruster actuation -> trajectory deviation at 2.1 km -> loss of attitude control -> hard landing.
missions.design_smell: Phase-transition (rough->fine braking) software not stress-tested in flight-representative integrated environment; single-string guidance with no graceful degradation; limited ability to handle parameter dispersion.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Hardware-in-the-loop testing of phase transitions; dispersion tolerance; redundancy (later applied in Chandrayaan-3)
missions.ility: Loss of robustness — limited ability to handle parameter variation/dispersion (per ISRO's own later assessment)
missions.key_quote: The Vikram craft 'hard landed' on the moon because of a problem with its braking thrusters. — Jitendra Singh, Indian Minister of State for the Dept. of Space
missions.source_title: India Admits Its Moon Lander Crashed, Cites Problem with Braking Thrusters (Space.com)
missions.confidence_note: ISRO never released a full public technical report; characterization comes from ministerial statements and summaries of an internal report.

missions.flight_phase: Autonomous descent + hover hazard avoidance; sample return
missions.sensor_or_actuator: L3DIS multi-beam lidar for real-time 3D terrain maps; ADDED a Navigation Doppler Lidar for precision velocity/altitude during descent
missions.contributing_logic: Same hover-and-scan hazard-avoidance lineage, with added sensor diversity: a Navigation Doppler Lidar (vibration/plume-tested pre-mission) supplemented radar for descent velocity/altitude.
missions.propagation_chain: Mature autonomous hazard-avoidance + added dissimilar Doppler-lidar sensing -> precise soft landing -> sample return.
missions.good_practices: Sensor diversity beyond radar (Doppler lidar added); pre-mission environmental testing of the new sensor.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Dissimilar redundant ranging/velocimetry; pre-flight environmental qualification
missions.key_quote: Chang'e-5 added a Navigation Doppler Lidar (flight/vibration/plume-tested) for precision velocity/altitude during descent.
missions.source_title: Remote Sensing review of Chang'e descent sensing (MDPI)

missions.sensor_or_actuator: Laser rangefinder / radar altimeter; onboard altitude-estimation filter
missions.proximate_cause: Flying over the steep rim of Atlas crater (~3 km elevation step), the laser altimeter correctly reported a large, rapid altitude increase. The onboard outlier-rejection filter classified the real terrain feature as a sensor malfunction and blocked the data; the internal altitude estimate froze near zero while true altitude was ~5 km. The lander 'thought' it had landed, descended slowly until propellant exhausted, then free-fell.
missions.contributing_logic: A filter designed to reject altitude readings that differ greatly from the pre-planned estimate had no terrain-aware check and no way to tell sensor noise from genuine terrain. Once it quarantined the only altitude source, GNC ran on a stale/incorrect estimate with no path to detect the persistent error. Contributing factor: the landing site was changed after CDR (Feb 2021) and prior simulations did not adequately incorporate the new route's terrain.
missions.propagation_chain: Planned route crosses crater rim (~3 km step) -> altimeter reports large (valid) altitude increase -> outlier filter rejects the data as anomalous -> GNC runs on altitude estimate ~0 -> lander hovers/descends believing it has landed at ~5 km true altitude -> propellant exhausted -> free-fall impact.
missions.design_smell: Single-string altimetry with hard outlier rejection, no terrain-aware sanity model, and no fused fallback; post-CDR landing-site change without full re-simulation of the new terrain corridor.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Terrain-aware sanity bounds (use a DEM along the route); dissimilar redundant altimetry with fusion; re-run full V&V after any post-CDR ConOps change
missions.ility: Loss of robustness — design could not maintain valid state estimation under a foreseeable terrain disturbance
missions.key_quote: The onboard software determined in error that the cause of this discrepancy was an abnormal value reported by the sensor, and thereafter the altitude data measured by the sensor was intercepted. — ispace official investigation, May 26, 2023
missions.source_title: ispace Announces Results of the 'HAKUTO-R' Mission 1 Lunar Landing

missions.sensor_or_actuator: BIUS-L angular-velocity/accelerometer unit; main engine (burn timer)
missions.proximate_cause: During the pre-landing correction burn, the control complex failed to power on the accelerometer within the BIUS-L unit, so it received zero velocity feedback and could not detect when target delta-V was reached. The burn fell back to a fixed time cutoff and fired ~127 s instead of the planned ~84 s, over-shooting delta-V; Luna 25 struck the Moon.
missions.contributing_logic: The BIUS-L power-on failure was traced to 'commands of differing priority entered into one data array,' i.e., a command-scheduling/priority conflict that displaced the enable command. There was no cross-check that BIUS-L was live before the irreversible burn, and the burn defaulted to timer-only when no feedback was present.
missions.propagation_chain: Command-priority conflict in a shared array -> BIUS-L accelerometer not powered on -> zero velocity feedback -> no delta-V cutoff -> engine runs 127 s vs 84 s -> excess velocity -> impact (~400 km off target).
missions.design_smell: Irreversible maneuver authorized with no verification that the primary feedback sensor is live; shared command array with priority ambiguity; timer-only fallback with no abort/warning when feedback is absent.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Pre-burn sensor-liveness cross-check + abort; deterministic command sequencing; do not silently fall back to open-loop timing on a critical burn
missions.ility: Loss of robustness/survivability — a latent software/command defect was not contained before an irreversible action
missions.key_quote: The on-board control complex received zero signals from the accelerometers of the BIUS-L device... as a result of which its shutdown occurred according to a temporary setting. — Roscosmos, Oct 3, 2023
missions.source_title: Russia blames Luna-25 crash on computer glitch (SpaceNews)
missions.confidence_note: Mechanism (127 s burn, BIUS-L not powered, command-priority conflict) from official Roscosmos statement; deeper systemic root cause not publicly addressed.

missions.sensor_or_actuator: One of two main descent engines (nozzle lost); attitude-control thrusters; autonomous GNC
missions.proximate_cause: At ~50 m altitude the nozzle of one of two main engines was lost, producing asymmetric thrust and ~9 m/s eastward lateral velocity that the attitude-control thrusters could not fully counter. The autonomous GNC detected the anomaly and continued on the remaining engine, but touchdown lateral velocity/attitude were out of range; the lander tipped nose-down with solar panels facing west.
missions.contributing_logic: The autonomous fault response was actually correct (it detected the engine-out and continued in degraded mode) — the tip-over was the physical consequence of unrecoverable lateral velocity. Pinpoint landing (~55-100 m) was achieved; the craft later recovered power when the Sun angle changed.
missions.propagation_chain: Nozzle structural failure at ~50 m -> asymmetric thrust -> ~9 m/s lateral velocity -> ACS cannot fully counter -> GNC continues in degraded mode -> touchdown at acceptable vertical but excess lateral velocity -> tips nose-down -> solar panels face west (no power) -> later recovered.
missions.design_smell: A single nozzle structural failure can tip the lander despite correct software; ACS not sized for engine-out lateral transient; power/solar geometry tightly coupled to an upright pose with no contingency path.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Size attitude control for engine-out transients; design power generation tolerant to off-nominal attitude (multi-face solar)
missions.ility: Strong survivability — a finite-duration disturbance degraded but did not destroy value delivery (pinpoint landing + data returned, later power recovery)
missions.key_quote: At an altitude of 50m... the thrust from one of the two main engines was most likely lost... SLIM onboard software autonomously identifies the anomaly, and while controlling the horizontal position as much as possible, SLIM continued the descent with the other engine. — JAXA, Jan 25, 2024
missions.source_title: Outcome for the Smart Lander for Investigating Moon (SLIM)'s Moon Landing (JAXA)
missions.confidence_note: Failure mode (nozzle loss, asymmetric thrust, tip) confirmed by JAXA; the physical cause of nozzle detachment was still under investigation (LOW confidence on that sub-cause).

missions.flight_phase: Post-separation propulsion commissioning (~1 h after launch)
missions.sensor_or_actuator: Pressure Control Valve 2 (PCV2) in the oxidizer helium pressurant line; oxidizer tank
missions.proximate_cause: During automated propulsion initialization, PCV2 failed to fully reseat after actuation; the root cause was vibration-initiated relaxation of an internal threaded joint that damaged the primary O-ring sealing surface. Uncontrolled helium flow over-pressurized and ruptured the oxidizer tank; the resulting leak tumbled the spacecraft and depleted propellant, making landing impossible.
missions.contributing_logic: No software fault. PCV2 had been flagged during integration (PCV1 had required repair; PCV2 was not repaired/replaced because it was inaccessible and replacement would have invalidated completed acceptance tests and missed the launch window). A risk-acceptance decision was made to fly with PCV2 as a known open item.
missions.propagation_chain: Threaded-joint relaxation from launch vibration -> O-ring sealing surface damaged -> PCV2 fails to reseat -> uncontrolled helium into oxidizer tank -> over-pressurization -> tank rupture -> continuous leak -> tumble + propellant loss -> mission abort -> controlled Earth reentry.
missions.design_smell: Known valve risk item flown unresolved under schedule/test-invalidation pressure; inaccessible propulsion component prevents last-chance inspection/swap; single PCV per propellant branch with no redundancy/isolation; no component-level vibration-seating test.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Resolve known risk items before flight (Owens & de Weck: redundancy beats hoping); redundant/isolatable pressurant valves; design for accessibility/inspectability
missions.ility: Loss of robustness — a known latent defect was not contained; no redundancy at the valve
missions.key_quote: PCV2 suffered a loss-of-seal capability, most likely due to a mechanical failure caused by vibration-initiated relaxation between threaded components internal to the valve. — Astrobotic PM1 Post-Mission Report (Aug 2024)
missions.source_title: Peregrine Mission One Post-Mission Report (Astrobotic, Aug 2024)
missions.confidence_note: Independent 34-member review board; failure mode replicated in ground testing.

missions.flight_phase: Terminal descent / final approach and touchdown
missions.subsystem: GNC / sensor (laser rangefinder safety switch) + software (NDL data-valid flag)
missions.sensor_or_actuator: Laser rangefinders (primary, disabled); NASA Navigation Doppler Lidar (NDL, backup); IMU + optical nav (final fallback)
missions.proximate_cause: After mating to the launch vehicle, a physical eye-safety interlock switch on the laser rangefinders was left armed and could not be disabled remotely, so the rangefinders were non-functional during descent. Engineers improvised using the NASA NDL payload, but the integration was incomplete — a data-valid flag was not set in software, so NDL data were silently rejected too. The lander descended on IMU + optical nav only, came in fast, caught a foot, and tipped to ~30 degrees.
missions.contributing_logic: Two-layer failure: (1) the eye-safety switch had no mandatory pre-encapsulation verification step; (2) the last-minute NDL workaround was not validated end-to-end (the data-valid flag was missed), so the nav filter received invalid data while engineers believed the fix worked. The in-flight software patch to use NDL at all was, separately, an impressive recovery.
missions.propagation_chain: Eye-safety switch left armed (ground error) -> laser rangefinders dead in descent -> last-minute NDL workaround -> NDL data-valid flag not set -> NDL data rejected -> descent on IMU + optical nav only -> excess velocity at touchdown -> foot catches -> tips to ~30 deg (survived, returned data).
missions.design_smell: Safety interlock not on a mandatory pre-encapsulation checklist (human-factors/process gap); last-minute sensor substitution with no end-to-end integration test; no automated check that at least one ranging sensor is valid before committing to powered descent.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Pre-flight checklist gates for safety interlocks; a no-go check that >=1 ranging sensor reports valid before descent; integration testing of any in-flight reconfiguration
missions.ility: Survivability (returned data despite tip) but a robustness failure in process/integration; a pre-flight test-fire would likely have caught the switch
missions.key_quote: While engineers had successfully mapped the data from the NASA payload into their software, they had missed a data flag in the software to let it know the data were valid. — Tim Crain, Intuitive Machines CTO
missions.source_title: Intuitive Machines' Odysseus lander tipped over on the moon during 'spicy' lunar landing (Space.com)
missions.confidence_note: Switch failure and NDL data-flag omission confirmed by IM's own post-mission briefings; touchdown dynamics inferred from telemetry/imagery.

missions.flight_phase: Autonomous visual obstacle avoidance + hover (~100 m) + laser 3D scan; far-side sample return
missions.sensor_or_actuator: Visible-light hazard camera; laser 3D scanner for fine obstacle detection during hover
missions.proximate_cause: None — first far-side sample return (SPA/Apollo basin).
missions.contributing_logic: Mature Chang'e hover-imaging pattern applied far-side: visible-light camera detects obstacles and selects a safe zone, then hover at ~100 m with a laser 3D scanner for fine detection before final descent.
missions.propagation_chain: Validated autonomous visual + laser hazard avoidance -> safe-site selection -> soft landing -> sample return.
missions.good_practices: Reuse of validated hazard-avoidance architecture; dissimilar sensing (camera + laser); hover margin.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Heritage + dissimilar terrain sensing + hover margin
missions.ility: Robustness via commonality and sensor diversity
missions.key_quote: Chang'e-6 used autonomous visual obstacle avoidance then hovered ~100 m and ran a laser 3D scanner for fine obstacle detection before final descent.

missions.flight_phase: Powered descent to south-polar region; explicit redesign after Chandrayaan-2
missions.sensor_or_actuator: Two hazard-detection cameras (vs one on C-2); ADDED Laser Doppler Velocimeter (independent velocity sensor); extra TTC antennas; removed central engine
missions.proximate_cause: None — succeeded by explicitly engineering against Chandrayaan-2's failure modes. THIS IS THE KEY VALIDATION CASE for the failure-memory methodology.
missions.contributing_logic: ISRO adopted a 'failure-based design' philosophy: (1) redundancy — two hazard cameras, an independent Laser Doppler Velocimeter for redundant velocity measurement, extra comms antennas; (2) software robustness — tolerant of engine disruptions, thrust dispersions, and sensor failures; (3) larger landing zone expanded from 500 m x 500 m to ~4 km x 2.5 km; (4) more fuel for divert; (5) stronger legs rated to higher impact velocity; (6) extended multi-face solar panels to survive off-nominal orientation.
missions.propagation_chain: Enumerate C-2 failure modes -> add redundancy + dispersion tolerance + larger margin -> robust descent -> soft landing (south-polar region).
missions.good_practices: A real-world instance of the exact methodology this tool advocates: enumerate prior failure modes and design against each, favoring redundancy and margin over a 'success-based' single-string design.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Failure-based design: redundancy + dissimilar sensors + dispersion tolerance + larger landing ellipse + survivable structure/power
missions.ility: Engineered-in robustness AND survivability (design tolerates dispersions; structure/power survive off-nominal pose)
missions.key_quote: Instead of success-based design in Chandrayaan-2, we are doing a failure-based design in Chandrayaan-3. What all can fail, and how to protect it — this is the approach that we have taken. — ISRO Chairman S. Somanath
missions.source_title: Chandrayaan-3 has a 'failure-based design', ISRO chief S. Somanath explains (DNA India)

missions.flight_phase: Autonomous powered descent with vision-based Terrain-Relative Navigation + Hazard Avoidance
missions.sensor_or_actuator: Two vision cameras on a dedicated processor (Vision Navigation Software) matching live imagery to onboard lunar maps at ~1 Hz; IMU; map-relative correction loop
missions.proximate_cause: None — first fully successful commercial lunar landing (Mare Crisium, near Mons Latreille).
missions.contributing_logic: Dual vision cameras estimate position/attitude by matching live imagery against preloaded/rendered lunar maps, continuously correcting the nav solution. In the final moments the system detected previously undocumented hazards and autonomously executed two hazard-avoidance divert maneuvers to a safe site.
missions.propagation_chain: Vision-based TRN + hazard avoidance (redundant cameras + IMU + map correction) -> detect undocumented hazards -> autonomous diverts -> on-target soft landing -> ~14 days of operations.
missions.good_practices: Redundant dissimilar navigation (dual cameras + IMU + map matching); autonomous hazard-relative navigation and divert capability; full terminal autonomy.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Terrain-relative navigation + redundant sensing + autonomous hazard divert
missions.ility: Robustness (held nav solution under undocumented hazards) + survivability (autonomous divert)
missions.key_quote: Blue Ghost used vision-based Terrain-Relative Navigation and autonomously executed two hazard-avoidance divert maneuvers to a safe site.
missions.source_title: Touchdown! Firefly's Blue Ghost Lands on the Moon (NASA)

missions.flight_phase: Terminal descent / powered landing at the lunar south pole
missions.subsystem: GNC / sensor (laser altimeter) + optical navigation (crater recognition) + terrain model
missions.sensor_or_actuator: Laser altimeter (noisy/distorted in descent); optical navigation camera (degraded by south-pole lighting); crater-recognition database built from 100 km LRO imagery
missions.proximate_cause: The laser altimeter saw signal noise/distortion that prevented accurate altitude readings, while low-angle south-pole sunlight created long shadows and dim lighting that degraded optical navigation. The crater-recognition database (from 100 km imagery) did not represent how craters appear at low altitude under that lighting. The lander misjudged position/descent rate, struck a plateau, tipped, and skidded into a crater ~250 m off target; shadowed and regolith-covered, it lost power within hours.
missions.contributing_logic: Crater-recognition tuning for south-pole geometry was inadequate (reference altitude/lighting mismatch). Altimeter noise handling left GNC without a reliable altitude reference in the final meters. No redundant dissimilar altimeter was present; IM said post-mission it would add one for IM-3. Lessons from IM-1's altimetry problems were not yet fully implemented.
missions.propagation_chain: Laser-altimeter distortion -> inaccurate altitude -> south-pole lighting degrades optical nav -> crater-recognition mismatch (100 km reference vs approach reality) -> nav error -> strikes plateau off-target -> tips and skids into crater -> regolith + shadow -> power depleted within hours.
missions.design_smell: Repeated single-string laser altimeter dependence (IM-1 -> IM-2) with no dissimilar backup; terrain/crater reference not validated against approach-geometry and south-pole lighting; standard optical-nav assumptions applied to extreme polar lighting.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Dissimilar redundant altimetry; validate terrain/crater databases against approach-angle + polar lighting; environment-specific nav tuning
missions.ility: Loss of both robustness (state estimation failed under polar conditions) and survivability (tipped, shadowed, dead within hours)
missions.key_quote: We saw signal noise and distortion that did not allow for accurate altitude readings. South Pole topography and low-angle sunlight created long shadows and dim lighting conditions that challenged the precision capability of our landing systems. — Steve Altemus, Intuitive Machines CEO
missions.source_title: Intuitive Machines' CEO points to issues that prevented upright touchdown during IM-2 (Spaceflight Now)
missions.confidence_note: Three contributing factors from official CEO statement + post-mission review; exact altimeter-distortion physics not elaborated (MEDIUM on that sub-cause).

missions.sensor_or_actuator: Laser Range Finder (LRF) — primary descent range/velocity sensor
missions.proximate_cause: The LRF under-performed in flight and did not return valid altitude measurements in time to trigger the final braking burn, so the lander could not slow to a safe landing speed. Communications were lost ~90 s before the projected landing; LRO later confirmed a crash. ispace ruled out the guidance software, propulsion, and power, attributing the loss to LRF hardware performance degradation (different from M1's software filter). [Press reporting of ispace's briefing indicated the LRF, intended to acquire from several km altitude, did not produce a valid reading until far lower; those specific acquisition altitudes are from secondary reporting, not ispace's primary statement.]
missions.contributing_logic: ispace's June 24, 2025 analysis found no install error and concluded in-flight LRF performance was degraded versus ground tests, narrowing to four candidate factors: (1) unexpected lunar-surface reflectivity, (2) laser incidence angle reducing return, (3) lower laser power, (4) reduced performance at high descent velocity / space-environment effects. The flown LRF was not flight-proven. No independent dissimilar ranging sensor covered an LRF shortfall. The CTO noted the crash site was very close to target, which actually validated the GNC algorithms — the failure was the sensor, not the guidance.
missions.propagation_chain: LRF in-flight performance degradation (not flight-proven unit) -> valid range not available in time to trigger braking -> braking burn triggered too late -> lander cannot decelerate to safe speed -> hard impact (Mare Frigoris).
missions.design_smell: SAME ARCHITECTURAL CLASS AS M1: a single-string terminal-descent laser-ranging sensor with no dissimilar redundant backup and no graceful degradation. M1 fixed the specific software bug but the architecture still had one ranging chain whose shortfall is loss-critical. Also: flying a not-flight-proven sensor for a loss-critical function.
missions.mitigation_pattern: Dissimilar redundant ranging with fusion + graceful degradation; fly flight-proven sensors for loss-critical functions; fix the failure CLASS (single-point ranging), not just the specific bug
missions.ility: Loss of robustness — a loss-critical single-string sensor's in-flight underperformance was not contained
missions.key_quote: The review concluded that the possibility of 'deterioration in the performance of the LRF during flight or the performance itself was lower than expected' is high. — ispace Technical Cause Analysis, June 24, 2025
missions.source_title: ispace Releases Technical Cause Analysis for HAKUTO-R Mission 2
missions.confidence_note: LRF hardware underperformance as proximate cause is official; the exact degradation mechanism was narrowed to four candidates, not definitively isolated (MEDIUM on the precise physics). The 'same architectural class as M1' framing is our abstraction; ispace stresses the proximate causes differ (software vs hardware).

design_checks.name: Single-string terminal-descent ranging sensor
design_checks.question: Does terminal descent depend on a single ranging/altimetry sensor (laser rangefinder, radar altimeter) with no DISSIMILAR redundant backup and no graceful degradation if it fails, is filtered out, or under-performs?
design_checks.recommendation: Provide a dissimilar redundant ranging path (e.g., Doppler lidar + radar/laser) with sensor fusion and explicit graceful degradation; redundancy is more cost-effective than chasing the reliability of a single string. Chandrayaan-3 added an independent Laser Doppler Velocimeter after Chandrayaan-2; Chang'e and Blue Ghost fly dissimilar sensors.
design_checks.deweck_hook: Owens & de Weck (2016): reliability growth alone is superlinear in test time and exponential in cost; combine with redundancy. de Weck et al. failure-propagation: a single-string loss-critical element is where cascades start.

design_checks.name: Hard outlier rejection without terrain-aware sanity model
design_checks.question: Does the navigation filter reject 'anomalous' sensor readings without a terrain-aware (DEM-based) sanity model, such that valid but surprising data (e.g., a crater-rim altitude step) can be discarded with no fallback?
design_checks.recommendation: Gate measurements against a terrain-aware expectation (DEM along the planned route), not just deviation from a pre-planned estimate; never let a filter silently quarantine the only source of a state with no fallback. Hakuto-R M1 rejected a valid 3 km crater-rim step and 'thought' it had landed at 5 km.
design_checks.deweck_hook: Norheim & de Weck (2024): LLMs/analysis are good at surfacing exactly this kind of logic gap in requirements/design text.

design_checks.name: No pre-maneuver sensor-liveness / validity check
design_checks.question: Before an irreversible maneuver (e.g., landing burn, orbit-lowering burn), does the system verify that its critical feedback sensors are powered, valid, and producing data — with an abort/hold if not — rather than silently falling back to open-loop timing or invalid data?
design_checks.recommendation: Require an explicit go/no-go that all loss-critical feedback sensors are live and reporting valid data before committing to any irreversible maneuver; never silently fall back to open-loop timing (Luna 25 burned 127 s vs 84 s on a timer because the accelerometer was never powered) or to data that is present but flagged invalid (IM-1's NDL data-valid flag).
design_checks.deweck_hook: de Weck failure-propagation: an unchecked precondition lets a latent defect drive an irreversible action.

design_checks.name: Sensor/recovery commands can disturb propulsion or trigger reboots
design_checks.question: Can a sensor-management action, fault response, or ground recovery command trigger a flight-computer reboot or otherwise shut down / disturb propulsion during a critical burn? Is the engine-control path isolated and latched through faults/reboots?
design_checks.recommendation: Isolate sensor-recovery and fault-management from the engine-control path; latch propulsion through reboots/fault transients during powered descent; validate fault management under COMBINED faults (hardware failure + telecommand). Beresheet's main engine shut down because a command to restart a failed IMU rebooted the computer.
design_checks.deweck_hook: Owens & de Weck: redundancy + containment over single-string fault paths.

design_checks.name: Terrain / hazard database not validated for approach geometry and lighting
design_checks.question: Is the terrain-relative-navigation / crater-recognition / hazard database validated against the ACTUAL approach geometry, altitude, and lighting (e.g., low-angle south-pole sun, long shadows), rather than nadir/high-altitude reference imagery?
design_checks.recommendation: Validate TRN/crater-recognition against approach-angle and site-specific lighting (model long shadows and dim low-sun conditions); do not rely on high-altitude nadir reference imagery for low-altitude polar approaches. IM-2 used 100 km reference imagery for a low-angle south-pole approach.
design_checks.deweck_hook: Epoch-Era / context sensitivity: validate the design in the actual operating context, not a convenient reference context.

design_checks.name: Known open risk item accepted under schedule/cost pressure
design_checks.question: Are there known open risk items, deferred fixes, inaccessible-for-inspection components, or skipped tests being carried to flight because resolving them would impact schedule/cost or invalidate prior acceptance tests?
design_checks.recommendation: Retire loss-critical risk items before flight; design for accessibility/inspectability; fly flight-proven hardware for loss-critical functions; do not let test-invalidation or schedule drive risk acceptance on single-string critical elements. Peregrine flew a known-suspect valve; IM-1 flew with an unverified safety switch; Hakuto-R M2 flew a not-flight-proven LRF.
design_checks.deweck_hook: Owens & de Weck: the cost-effective path is redundancy/commonality + retiring risk, not accepting single-string risk and hoping.

design_checks.name: Post-CDR ConOps / landing-site change without full re-validation
design_checks.question: Has the concept of operations, landing site, descent route, or a sensor/payload configuration changed after Critical Design Review WITHOUT a full re-run of simulation/V&V for the new conditions?
design_checks.recommendation: Any post-CDR change to landing site, route, or sensor configuration must trigger a full re-run of terrain/descent simulation and V&V. Hakuto-R M1 changed its landing site after CDR and did not adequately re-simulate the new route's terrain (the crater rim that triggered the altitude-filter rejection).
design_checks.deweck_hook: de Weck change-management / Delta-DSM: assess the invasiveness and downstream re-validation needs of any late change.

design_checks.name: Power/comms survival not robust to off-nominal landing attitude
design_checks.question: If the lander ends up in an off-nominal attitude (tipped, on its side), can it still generate power and communicate long enough to be useful? Or is power/comms tightly coupled to a single upright pose?
design_checks.recommendation: Provide power generation and comms paths that tolerate off-nominal attitude (e.g., solar cells on multiple faces, as Chandrayaan-3 added). SLIM survived a tip because of later illumination recovery; IM-2 died within hours because it tipped into shadow with covered panels.
design_checks.deweck_hook: de Weck survivability (CESUN 2012): minimize the impact of a finite-duration disturbance on value delivery — a tip-over should degrade, not end, the mission.

design_checks.name: Attitude control not sized for engine-out / asymmetric-thrust transients
design_checks.question: Is the attitude-control authority sized to handle an engine-out or asymmetric-thrust event during terminal descent (lateral velocity build-up), and is the structure (legs) rated for the resulting off-nominal touchdown?
design_checks.recommendation: Size attitude-control authority and leg strength for engine-out/asymmetric-thrust transients in terminal descent. SLIM lost a nozzle at 50 m and tipped from unrecoverable lateral velocity despite a correct software response; Chandrayaan-3 added stronger legs and removed its troublesome central engine.
design_checks.deweck_hook: de Weck failure-propagation: a propulsion fault propagates to attitude then to landing geometry unless contained by margin.
